ocr.text: In the context of the ongoing evolution of aquaculture practices in Europe, the data presented in the accompanying harvest table
provides critical insights into production metrics for key species. Notably, the total production of salmon in Europe reached an
impressive 20,000 metric tons, underscoring the region's significant role in global aquaculture.
Region  Species  ProductionVolume  Revenue(€M)  NetIncome(€M)  CAPEX(€M)  OperatingMargin(%)  GrowthRate(%)
Europe  Salmon  20000  150  45  15  30  5
Europe  Tilapia  18000  130  40  12  25  4
Europe  Catfish  15000  90  25  10  30  3
Europe  Shrimp  25000  200  60  [HIDDEN]  40  7
Europe  Trout  12000  70  20  8  15  2
Europe  Bass  8000  [HIDDEN]  15  5  10  1
Europe  Pangasius  [HIDDEN]  110  30  9  20  3
Europe  Cod  9000  60  18  6  12  2
Europe  Mackerel  7000  40  12  4  8  1
Europe  Sardines  5000  30  10  3  6  1
Europe  Flatfish  3000  20  5  2  [HIDDEN]  0.5
The data reveals that the average weight of harvested salmon was 150 grams, which aligns with consumer preferences for
portion-sized fish. Furthermore, the report indicates that the survival rate of the harvested fish was 45%, reflecting
advancements in breeding and feeding techniques that contribute to healthier stocks. The economic implications of these figures
are noteworthy; for instance, the revenue generated from these harvests is estimated at EUR 16,000, a substantial contribution
to the European aquaculture sector. Additionally, the feed conversion ratio, a critical efficiency metric, was reported at 15,
which highlights the effectiveness of current feeding practices. The production process also faced challenges, as indicated by a
mortality rate of 20%, which necessitates ongoing research and intervention strategies to mitigate losses. Overall, these figures
provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of salmon aquaculture in Europe, emphasizing both the potential for
growth and the areas requiring further attention. The industry's ability to adapt and thrive in a competitive market will depend
on continuous improvements in sustainability and resource management practices.
